Client 2: Net is like… maybe sixty-ish?
Advisor: Okay—hang on—gross before taxes, not what hits your checking. Do you know the gross annual total, roughly?
Client 1: Yeah, gross is about sixty-three, seven. Around $63,700 a year.
Client 2: Right, I was thinking take-home. Sorry.
Advisor: No worries at all—happens constantly. And within that, Davi, you’ve got some self-employed income that comes in quarterly, right?

Advisor: Great. Let me jot that. Now expenses—what do you two spend per month, all-in, not counting any debt payments?
Client 2: About two thousand a month—give or take.
Client 1: It’s a bit higher. It’s more like $2,200 a month.
Advisor: Okay, so $2,200 monthly expenses. Linda, when you said two thousand, was that excluding something like insurance or property stuff?
Client 2: Yeah—excluding a couple things. Davi’s right, call it $2,200.

Client 2: Net is like… maybe sixty-ish?
Advisor: Okay—hang on—gross before taxes, not what hits your checking. Do you know the gross annual total, roughly?
Client 1: Yeah, gross is about sixty-three, seven. Around $63,700 a year.
Client 2: Right, I was thinking take-home. Sorry.
Advisor: No worries at all—happens constantly. And within that, Davi, you’ve got some self-employed income that comes in quarterly, right?

Advisor: So monthly debt cost is zero. Good. Now I want to shift to assets—investable first, then the house. Davi, what do you consider your total investable assets across accounts?
Client 1: Roughly two hundred thousand? Maybe two-ten?
Advisor: Okay—ballpark. If we tighten it up, is it about $215,400 total investable?
Client 1: Yes, that’s the number—about $215,400.
Client 2: That sounds right.

Client 2: That one makes me nervous, honestly.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll talk through guardrails on that. Now, separate from investable assets, your primary residence—what’s the value you’re using for that right now?
Client 1: About $102,100.
Client 2: Wait—no, that’s not what Zillow says—
Client 1: Linda—hang on—Zillow’s higher, but we’re using the conservative number we discussed. About $102,100.

Client 2: Net is like… maybe sixty-ish?
Advisor: Okay—hang on—gross before taxes, not what hits your checking. Do you know the gross annual total, roughly?
Client 1: Yeah, gross is about sixty-three, seven. Around $63,700 a year.

Advisor: No worries at all—happens constantly. And within that, Davi, you’ve got some self-employed income that comes in quarterly, right?

Client 1: Yeah, gross is about sixty-three, seven. Around $63,700 a year.
Client 2: Right, I was thinking take-home. Sorry.
Advisor: No worries at all—happens constantly. And within that, Davi, you’ve got some self-employed income that comes in quarterly, right?

Client 1: Thanks. I’m 63, trying to line up retirement around 65, and I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: And I’m still working—probably to 68—so I’m mostly worried about the month-to-month staying smooth.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it practical. Quick housekeeping: in my notes I’m labeling you as Client 1 and Client 2—Davi, you’re Client 1, and Linda, you’re Client 2. That right?
Client 1: Yes, I’m Client 1.
Client 2: Yep, I’m Client 2.

Advisor: Hi Davi, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m your advisor for today. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m 63, trying to line up retirement around 65, and I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: And I’m still working—probably to 68—so I’m mostly worried about the month-to-month staying smooth.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it practical. Quick housekeeping: in my notes I’m labeling you as Client 1 and Client 2—Davi, you’re Client 1, and Linda, you’re Client 2. That right?

Client 2: I always get confused—like, is that what we actually pay, or…
Advisor: Totally fair. Bracket is the “top slice” rate, not what you pay on every dollar. Davi, do you know roughly where you land?
Client 1: We’re in the 32% bracket.
Client 2: I thought it was 24—
Client 1: No, Linda, last year it pushed into 32%.

Client 2: More the illiquid stuff. The swings I can handle if we have a plan.
Client 1: Exactly—guardrails, not zero risk.
Advisor: Makes sense. One more admin-ish thing—what market are we operating in for your planning relationship? Like, are you working with a US-based RIA?
Client 2: I’m not sure what RIA means. Is that like the brokerage?
Advisor: Good catch—sorry. RIA just means a registered investment advisor here in the US, versus, say, a bank channel. For you, it’s US RIA—does that sound right?

Advisor: Hi Davi, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m your advisor for today. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m 63, trying to line up retirement around 65, and I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: And I’m still working—probably to 68—so I’m mostly worried about the month-to-month staying smooth.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it practical. Quick housekeeping: in my notes I’m labeling you as Client 1 and Client 2—Davi, you’re Client 1, and Linda, you’re Client 2. That right?
Client 1: Yes, I’m Client 1.

Client 2: Uh—well, yes, two adults.
Client 1: Correct, two adults.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, risk-wise—when markets get choppy, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, aggressive… where do you land?
Client 1: Moderate.
Client 2: I’d say moderate too—maybe a little cautious, but yeah, moderate.

Client 1: Yeah—sounds good.
Advisor: Thanks, Davi. Okay—going back to that earlier point about filing location and taxes, I want to make sure I’ve got the household basics right before we build scenarios.
Advisor: First one’s simple: are you two married, or partnered, or—how do you describe it officially?
Client 2: We’re married.
Client 1: Yeah, married.

Client 1: Yeah, it’s taxable, and it’s through an insurance provider. Value is about $132,450.
Client 2: That’s the big one, yeah.
Advisor: And Linda, you’ve got a retirement account—401(k) or IRA—also sitting with an insurance provider, around $39,200?
Client 2: Yes, about $39,200.
Advisor: Then you two have a bank-type account—joint—also showing as an insurance provider on the statement, about $19,350. Does that sound right?

Client 1: Yeah, gross is about sixty-three, seven. Around $63,700 a year.
Client 2: Right, I was thinking take-home. Sorry.
Advisor: No worries at all—happens constantly. And within that, Davi, you’ve got some self-employed income that comes in quarterly, right?
Client 1: Yeah, it’s basically quarterly—salary-ish—about $36,850 annualized.
Advisor: And then there’s also a monthly bonus component for you?

Client 1: Yes, private markets, about $24,450. And yeah, it’s not something I can just sell tomorrow.
Client 2: That one makes me nervous, honestly.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll talk through guardrails on that. Now, separate from investable assets, your primary residence—what’s the value you’re using for that right now?
Client 1: About $102,100.
Client 2: Wait—no, that’s not what Zillow says—

Advisor: Perfect.
Advisor: Now, Davi, I’m going to ask something a little personal but just for planning—your date of birth. Month, day, year?
Client 1: November 9th, 1962.
Client 2: Wait—1963? No, sorry—yeah, it’s 1962. I always do that.
Advisor: No worries—so November 9, 1962 for you, Davi.

Client 1: Yeah—sounds good.
Advisor: Alright—thanks. Going back to what you said earlier about no liabilities, that’s helpful.
Advisor: Let’s tighten up your work details a bit. Davi, what would you call your occupation group—like the broad bucket? Operations, sales, finance, that kind of thing.
Client 1: Operations, yeah.
Client 2: I’d say operations too—he’s not, like, sales.

Advisor: Hi Davi, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m your advisor for today. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m 63, trying to line up retirement around 65, and I just want to make sure we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: And I’m still working—probably to 68—so I’m mostly worried about the month-to-month staying smooth.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it practical. Quick housekeeping: in my notes I’m labeling you as Client 1 and Client 2—Davi, you’re Client 1, and Linda, you’re Client 2. That right?

Advisor: Okay, $19,600 annualized for the monthly bonus for you, Davi. Linda, you also have a monthly bonus line too, right?
Client 2: Yeah, mine’s smaller—about $7,250 a year, spread monthly.
Advisor: And your base pay, Linda—do we have that as about $24,150 gross annually?
Client 2: Yes, around $24,150.
